refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption in a form that allows stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) to assess whether operational practices meet the target. The target achievement is a validation concern dependent on operational execution, not system design. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what must be measured and communicated) and removes the solution-dependent assumption that the system itself 'achieves' the target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh, fuel volume, or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) during operation, and that these data are accessible in a form suitable for calculating energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved. Confirm that reporting interfaces (dashboard, API, log export) present this metric to authorized stakeholders within the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log energy consumption data during operation.
The NGHE solution shall acquire and log work output data (quantity and type of work performed) during operation.
The NGHE solution shall compute the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work) from logged data.
The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metrics and supporting data to authorized stakeholders for review and analysis.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the equipment control loop; calculate energy_per_ton continuously during operation and store snapshots at defined intervals.
DP3: Use equipment telemetry (OEM-provided energy/fuel consumption data) combined with independent work measurement; reconcile and compute energy_per_ton offline.

DP4: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: primary channel measures energy at the source (fuel tank, power meter) and work at the point of load (scale, displacement sensor); secondary channel cross-validates via equipment diagnostics; compute energy_per_ton with redundancy and error bounds.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: primary channel measures energy at the source (fuel tank, power meter) and work at the point of load (scale, displacement sensor); secondary channel cross-validates via equipment diagnostics; compute energy_per_ton with redundancy and error bounds.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and work measurement remain logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor types, different data streams) → low coupling.
Dual-channel design decouples primary measurement from validation logic, reducing single-point-of-failure risk.
Computation of energy_per_ton is a pure function of two independent inputs (energy, work) → no hidden dependencies.
Risk: If energy and work sensors share a common power supply or communication bus, physical coupling could emerge at IM layer; mitigation: specify independent power and data paths in IM design.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is preferred over DP1–DP3 because it balances measurement independence with validation robustness. DP1 is too passive (no real-time feedback for operational control). DP2 couples measurement to control logic, risking measurement bias. DP3 introduces vendor lock-in and trust assumptions on OEM telemetry. DP4 avoids these by keeping measurement channels logically separate and adding a validation layer.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel or electrical) can be instrumented without disrupting normal operation. Risk: Retrofit constraints on existing equipment may limit sensor placement; mitigation: conduct site survey to identify accessible measurement points.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage) can be measured independently and continuously. Risk: Scales or displacement sensors may have calibration drift or environmental sensitivity (mud, vibration). Mitigation: specify sensor redundancy and periodic calibration protocol.

Assumption: Equipment diagnostics (secondary channel) are available and trustworthy. Risk: OEM telemetry may have latency, quantization, or systematic bias. Mitigation: DP4 treats diagnostics as validation only, not primary measurement.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and establish statistical confidence in energy_per_ton ≤ E_target. Risk: Short evaluation windows or non-representative operating conditions may yield false negatives. Mitigation: define minimum evaluation duration and operational envelope in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Data logging and computation infrastructure can operate reliably in harsh worksite environment (dust, vibration, temperature extremes). Risk: Data loss or corruption. Mitigation: specify redundant logging (local + cloud) and error-detection codes.

Assumption: Stakeholders agree on the definition of 'work performed' (e.g., ton-km vs. ton-moved). Risk: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria. Mitigation: clarify and document in refined FR before IM design.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed, with dual-channel architecture (primary: direct source and load measurement; secondary: equipment diagnostics validation), such that the derived metric energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_work_performed satisfies ≤ E_target over a minimum evaluation period of [X days/weeks] under representative operational conditions, with measurement uncertainty ≤ [Y%] and data availability ≥ [Z%]. Acceptance criteria shall include calibration and validation protocols for all sensors and a documented reconciliation procedure for discrepancies between primary and secondary channels.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture module responsible for dual-channel energy and work quantification, cross-validation logic, redundancy management, and error-bound computation for energy_per_ton metric.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed energy_per_ton values, error bounds, and channel validation status to external monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive measurement configuration parameters (sensor calibration, redundancy thresholds, validation rules) from task orchestration

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts when measurement discrepancies exceed error bounds or channel failures are detected

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receive raw sensor data streams from Vehicle (fuel tank, power meter, scale, displacement sensor) and equipment diagnostics; may also query remote validation services

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy-efficiency measurement logic. Dual-channel architecture, cross-validation, and error-bound computation are core to NGHE's purpose. Primary diffusion risk: raw sensor data acquisition logic resides in Vehicle module (acceptable boundary); secondary risk: SafetyAlert integration requires external interface but is minimal.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns sensor hardware and data acquisition, but measurement logic, cross-validation, and energy_per_ton computation are not vehicle-specific concerns. Placing DP here would scatter measurement logic across vehicle and NGHE, violating cohesion. High diffusion of validation and redundancy logic.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is concerned with remote control and operator interface, not measurement architecture. Placing DP here would require extensive coupling to Vehicle (sensor data), NGHE (energy logic), and Safety Services (alerts). Severe diffusion.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns alert generation and safety policy enforcement, but the measurement architecture itself (dual-channel logic, error bounds, cross-validation) is not a safety service concern. Placing DP here conflates measurement with safety response. High diffusion of measurement logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The dual-channel measurement architecture is fundamentally an energy-efficiency quantification concern. NGHE Solution is the designated module for energy-per-ton computation and validation. By centralizing DP4 in NGHE Solution, all measurement logic, redundancy management, and error-bound computation remain cohesive. Vehicle module provides raw sensor data via Network Link interface; Safety Services consumes alerts via SafetyAlert interface. This separation preserves single responsibility and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Sensor data acquisition timing and synchronization between primary and secondary channels must be coordinated with Vehicle module; latency or jitter could degrade cross-validation fidelity. Mitigation: define strict timing contracts in Network Link interface specification.

Equipment diagnostics (secondary channel) may require proprietary protocols or vendor-specific APIs not yet abstracted. If diagnostics logic becomes complex, consider a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution for diagnostic integration.

Error-bound thresholds and validation rules may need dynamic reconfiguration based on operational context (e.g., terrain, load type). Ensure Tasking interface supports runtime parameter updates without requiring code changes.

SafetyAlert interface coupling: if safety policy logic evolves, alert generation criteria may need revision. Establish clear contract for alert semantics to avoid tight coupling to Safety Services implementation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR by specifying a dual-channel measurement architecture (primary: energy at source + work at load; secondary: equipment diagnostics cross-validation) with explicit error-bound computation for energy_per_ton. The DP is solution-agnostic and logically sound: it defines the measurement strategy without prescribing implementation details. Traceability to acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' is clear—the DP enables quantification of the metric and validation of bounds.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data acquisition timing and synchronization: Vehicle module owns raw sensor streams (fuel tank, power meter, scale, displacement sensor); NGHE Solution owns cross-validation logic. Temporal alignment between primary and secondary channels is critical for fidelity. Risk: if timing contracts in Network Link interface are not rigorously specified, jitter or latency skew could invalidate cross-validation. Mitigation: define strict synchronization SLAs (e.g., max clock drift, sampling phase alignment) in Network Link interface specification.

Equipment diagnostics integration complexity: secondary channel relies on proprietary vendor APIs or protocols for equipment diagnostics. If diagnostic data parsing, error handling, or vendor-specific logic becomes substantial, it may diffuse across NGHE Solution and external diagnostic adapters. Risk: tight coupling to vendor implementations. Mitigation: create a dedicated sub-module (e.g., DiagnosticsAdapter) within NGHE Solution to abstract vendor protocols and isolate diffusion.

Dynamic reconfiguration of error-bound thresholds and validation rules: Tasking interface must support runtime parameter updates (sensor calibration, redundancy thresholds, validation rules) without code changes. Risk: if configuration logic is scattered between Tasking interface handler and core measurement logic, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: centralize all configuration state management in a single Configuration sub-module within NGHE Solution; Tasking interface updates only this module.

SafetyAlert interface coupling: NGHE Solution emits alerts when measurement discrepancies exceed bounds or channel failures occur. Risk: if alert semantics or thresholds are tightly coupled to Safety Services implementation details, changes to safety policy could require measurement logic revision. Mitigation: define a clear, stable contract for SafetyAlert (e.g., alert type, severity, measurement context) that decouples measurement logic from safety response policy.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for DP4 (containment score 0.92, diffusion score 0.08). The dual-channel measurement architecture, cross-validation logic, redundancy management, and error-bound computation are fundamentally energy-efficiency quantification concerns—core to NGHE's purpose. Vehicle module correctly owns raw sensor data acquisition and hardware interfaces; this is an acceptable boundary diffusion because sensor data is a commodity input, not measurement logic. Teleoperation and Safety Services are correctly excluded (high diffusion scores 0.85 and 0.7 respectively). The IM boundary is well-scoped: all measurement logic, validation, and redundancy management are centralized in NGHE Solution, preserving cohesion and single responsibility. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are minimal and well-defined, reducing coupling risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' is directly measurable from DP4 outputs (computed energy_per_ton values with error bounds). NGHE Solution's Telemetry interface transmits energy_per_ton and error bounds to external monitoring/logging systems, enabling independent verification. Cross-validation status and channel validation results are also transmitted, allowing auditors to assess measurement confidence. Worksite safety plan compliance is verified via SafetyAlert interface: alerts are emitted when measurement discrepancies exceed bounds, providing evidence of safety-critical monitoring. Verification is feasible provided: (1) Network Link interface delivers synchronized sensor data with documented latency/jitter characteristics; (2) Telemetry interface includes timestamped, auditable logs of all energy_per_ton computations and validation decisions; (3) error-bound thresholds are documented and traceable to operational requirements.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated ownership of DP4. Responsibilities are explicitly partitioned: NGHE Solution owns measurement logic, cross-validation, and error-bound computation; Vehicle module owns sensor hardware and raw data acquisition; Safety Services owns alert response policy (not measurement logic). Interfaces are named and directional (Telemetry out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout), enabling clear allocation of data flow and control. However, allocation clarity depends on rigorous interface specifications: Network Link must define sensor data schema, timing guarantees, and error handling; Tasking must define configuration parameter schema and update semantics; SafetyAlert must define alert type taxonomy and severity levels. Recommend documenting these interface contracts in a separate Interface Control Document (ICD) to ensure allocation clarity is maintained during implementation.

recommended_revisions: Specify Network Link interface contract in detail: define sensor data schema (fuel tank level, power meter reading, scale weight, displacement sensor output), sampling rate, maximum acceptable latency/jitter, clock synchronization mechanism, and error handling (e.g., sensor failure, data corruption). This is critical for cross-validation fidelity.

Create a DiagnosticsAdapter sub-module within NGHE Solution to abstract equipment diagnostics protocols and vendor-specific APIs. This isolates diffusion of vendor-specific logic and simplifies future vendor transitions.

Establish a Configuration sub-module within NGHE Solution to centralize management of sensor calibration, redundancy thresholds, and validation rules. Tasking interface updates only this module; core measurement logic reads configuration state without direct coupling to Tasking.

Define SafetyAlert interface contract: specify alert type taxonomy (e.g., 'CHANNEL_FAILURE', 'DISCREPANCY_EXCEEDED', 'CALIBRATION_DRIFT'), severity levels, and required context fields (e.g., measurement timestamp, error magnitude, affected channel). This decouples measurement logic from safety response policy.

Document error-bound computation algorithm and threshold derivation in DP4 specification. Ensure thresholds are traceable to acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' and operational safety requirements. Include sensitivity analysis: how do error bounds scale with sensor accuracy, sampling rate, and load variability?

Add acceptance test plan to DP4: specify how dual-channel cross-validation will be validated (e.g., inject known discrepancies, verify alert generation; compare primary and secondary channel outputs under nominal and fault conditions). This ensures DP4 is verifiable before implementation.

Consider adding a Telemetry interface specification that includes audit logging: all energy_per_ton computations, validation decisions, and error-bound calculations must be logged with timestamps and traceability to input sensor data. This supports post-hoc verification and forensic analysis.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP4 is logically sound, architecturally well-scoped to NGHE Solution, and directly verifiable against the acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target'. However, the gate analysis identifies four critical diffusion risks and recommends seven specific revisions that require sub-level decomposition to resolve: (1) sensor data synchronization and timing contracts (Network Link interface); (2) equipment diagnostics integration complexity (DiagnosticsAdapter sub-module); (3) dynamic configuration management (Configuration sub-module); (4) SafetyAlert interface decoupling. These are not defects in DP4 itself, but rather architectural packaging and interface design concerns that must be addressed at the next decomposition level to ensure implementation feasibility and risk containment. Proceeding directly to implementation without sub-level decomposition would defer critical interface and module boundary decisions, increasing rework risk. Safety criticality (high) reinforces the need for rigorous sub-level specification of measurement logic, cross-validation, and error-bound computation before coding begins.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Acquire and synchronize dual-channel sensor data (primary: energy at source + work at load; secondary: equipment diagnostics) with bounded latency and jitter to enable cross-validation fidelity.
FR4.2: Compute energy_per_ton from synchronized primary-channel sensor data (fuel consumption, power output, load weight, displacement) with documented error-bound propagation.
FR4.3: Validate primary-channel energy_per_ton against secondary-channel equipment diagnostics cross-validation results and emit SafetyAlert when discrepancies exceed error bounds.
FR4.4: Manage sensor calibration, redundancy thresholds, and validation rule parameters dynamically via Tasking interface without code changes.
FR4.5: Abstract equipment diagnostics vendor protocols and APIs to isolate vendor-specific logic and enable future vendor transitions.

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but lacks explicit sub-level decomposition of the dual-channel measurement architecture. Recommended revisions from gate analysis (Network Link interface contract, DiagnosticsAdapter sub-module, Configuration sub-module, SafetyAlert interface contract, error-bound algorithm documentation, acceptance test plan, audit logging) should be incorporated as sub-level DPs corresponding to FR4.1–FR4.5. This ensures that interface contracts, module boundaries, and verification strategies are specified before implementation, reducing diffusion risk and rework.

assumptions: Network Link interface will deliver synchronized sensor data with latency/jitter characteristics documented in a separate Interface Control Document (ICD).
Equipment diagnostics data is available via vendor APIs or protocols that can be abstracted into a dedicated DiagnosticsAdapter sub-module.
Tasking interface supports runtime parameter updates (sensor calibration, redundancy thresholds, validation rules) without requiring code changes.
SafetyAlert interface contract (alert type taxonomy, severity levels, context fields) can be defined independently of Safety Services implementation details.
Error-bound computation algorithm is deterministic and traceable to sensor accuracy specifications and operational safety requirements.
NGHE Solution has exclusive ownership of measurement logic, cross-validation, and error-bound computation; Vehicle module owns raw sensor data acquisition; Safety Services owns alert response policy.
open_risks: Sensor data acquisition timing and synchronization: Vehicle module owns raw sensor streams; NGHE Solution owns cross-validation logic. Temporal alignment between primary and secondary channels is critical. Risk: if timing contracts in Network Link interface are not rigorously specified, jitter or latency skew could invalidate cross-validation. Mitigation: define strict synchronization SLAs (max clock drift, sampling phase alignment) in Network Link ICD.

Equipment diagnostics integration complexity: secondary channel relies on proprietary vendor APIs. Risk: if diagnostic data parsing, error handling, or vendor-specific logic becomes substantial, it may diffuse across NGHE Solution and external diagnostic adapters. Mitigation: create dedicated DiagnosticsAdapter sub-module within NGHE Solution to abstract vendor protocols.

Dynamic reconfiguration of error-bound thresholds and validation rules: Tasking interface must support runtime parameter updates without code changes. Risk: if configuration logic is scattered between Tasking interface handler and core measurement logic, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: centralize all configuration state management in dedicated Configuration sub-module.

SafetyAlert interface coupling: NGHE Solution emits alerts when measurement discrepancies exceed bounds. Risk: if alert semantics are tightly coupled to Safety Services implementation, changes to safety policy could require measurement logic revision. Mitigation: define clear, stable SafetyAlert contract (alert type, severity, measurement context) that decouples measurement logic from safety response policy.

Error-bound threshold derivation: thresholds must be traceable to acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' and operational safety requirements. Risk: if thresholds are set arbitrarily or without sensitivity analysis, measurement confidence may be insufficient. Mitigation: document error-bound computation algorithm and include sensitivity analysis (how error bounds scale with sensor accuracy, sampling rate, load variability).

Audit logging and forensic traceability: Telemetry interface must include timestamped, auditable logs of all energy_per_ton computations and validation decisions. Risk: if logging is incomplete or non-deterministic, post-hoc verification and forensic analysis may be infeasible. Mitigation: specify audit logging requirements in Telemetry interface contract.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification (ICD): sensor data schema, sampling rate, maximum acceptable latency/jitter, clock synchronization mechanism, error handling for sensor failure/data corruption.
Equipment diagnostics vendor API documentation and protocol specifications to inform DiagnosticsAdapter design.
Tasking interface specification: configuration parameter schema, update semantics, validation rules for parameter changes.
SafetyAlert interface contract: alert type taxonomy, severity levels, required context fields (measurement timestamp, error magnitude, affected channel).
Error-bound computation algorithm specification: mathematical derivation, threshold values, sensitivity analysis (error bounds vs. sensor accuracy, sampling rate, load variability).

Acceptance test plan for DP4: test cases for dual-channel cross-validation (inject known discrepancies, verify alert generation; compare primary and secondary channel outputs under nominal and fault conditions).
Audit logging specification for Telemetry interface: log schema, timestamp precision, traceability to input sensor data, retention policy.
Verification plan for acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target': how will computed energy_per_ton values be independently verified? What is the audit trail for error-bound calculations?
